claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my child Alex, who is 19 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 19 years old, Child Benefit eligibility depends on their current situation. Child Benefit usually stops when a child turns 16, but can continue until their 20th birthday if they remain in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently in full-time, non-advanced education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or NVQs up to level 3) or on an approved training course?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in sixth form college studying for A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know, thank you. A-levels at sixth form college is considered approved education, which is good for eligibility purposes.

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Just a couple more questions to confirm eligibility.

**Are you responsible for Alex's day-to-day care, such as providing food, clothing, and accommodation?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for all of Alex's day-to-day needs - food, clothing, and accommodation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One more important question:

**Do you or Alex have any restrictions on your immigration status, or are you both UK residents with the right to live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We're both UK residents with the right to live in the UK. No restrictions on our immigration status.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all the information. Based on the details you've shared and the official guidance, I can now make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's a summary of why:

- ✅ **Age & Education**: Although Alex is 19 (above the usual cut-off of 16), Child Benefit can continue until age 20 as long as they remain in approved education. A-levels at sixth form college qualifies as approved, non-advanced education.

- ✅ **Living arrangements**: Alex lives with you.

- ✅ **Responsibility**: You are responsible for Alex's day-to-day care, including food, clothing, and accommodation.

- ✅ **Residency**: You are both UK residents with the right to live in the UK.

You should be able to claim or continue receiving Child Benefit for Alex until they either complete their A-levels, turn 20, or leave approved education/training—whichever comes first.

If you haven't already, you can apply for Child Benefit through the official government channels. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old, which is above the usual cut-off age of 16 for Child Benefit. However, Child Benefit can continue until a child turns 20 if they remain in approved education or training. Alex is studying A-levels at a sixth form college, which qualifies as approved full-time, non-advanced education. Additionally: (1) Alex lives with the claimant, (2) the claimant is responsible for Alex's day-to-day care including food, clothing, and accommodation, and (3) both the claimant and Alex are UK residents with the right to live in the UK and no immigration restrictions. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is 19 years old, they remain in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college), which extends eligibility until age 20. The claimant meets all other requirements: they are responsible for Alex, Alex lives with them, and they have the right to reside in the UK.

Additionally: (1) Alex lives with the claimant, (2) the claimant is responsible for Alex's day-to-day care including food, clothing, and accommodation, and (3) both the claimant and Alex are UK residents with the right to live in the UK and no immigration restrictions. All eligibility criteria are met.
